bibliographic_data.title: Approximation via Correlation Decay when Strong Spatial Mixing Fails
bibliographic_data.abstract: Approximate counting via correlation decay is the core algorithmic technique used in the sharp delineation of the computational phase transition that arises in the approximation of the partition function of anti-ferromagnetic two-spin models. Previous analyses of correlation-decay algorithms implicitly depended on the occurrence of strong spatial mixing. This paper develops a new analysis method that is more refined than worst-case analysis, taking into account the shape of instances in the computation tree and amortizing against certain 'bad' instances created during recursion. This enables us to show correlation decay and obtain an FPTAS even when strong spatial mixing fails. The technique applies to the problem of approximately counting independent sets in hypergraphs with degree upper-bound ∆ and a lower bound k on the arity of hyperedges, providing tight results for ∆ = 6 and k ≥ 3.

core_content.novelty_claim: Develops a new analysis method that enables correlation decay and FPTAS even when strong spatial mixing fails.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors take the shape of instances in the computation tree into consideration and amortize against certain 'bad' instances created during recursion, providing a more refined analysis than worst-case analysis.
outcomes: Shows correlation decay for hypergraphs with degree upper-bound ∆ and lower bound k on the arity of hyperedges even when strong spatial mixing fails.
Provides an FPTAS for counting independent sets in hypergraphs with ∆ = 6 and k ≥ 3.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper develops a new method for analyzing correlation decays in spin systems, specifically taking into account the shape of instances in the computation tree and amortizing against certain 'bad' instances created during recursion. This enables showing correlation decay and obtaining an FPTAS even when strong spatial mixing fails. The technique applies to approximately counting independent sets in hypergraphs with degree upper-bound ∆ and a lower bound k on the arity of hyperedges, providing tight results for ∆ = 6 and k ≥ 3.
